---
title: "On Listening Slowly"
description: "Some music only gives itself up over time. The question is whether we still know how to wait."
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2025-01-05"
categories:
  - Listening
  - Essays
---

## The problem with the first hearing

The first time I heard Morton Feldman's *For Philip Guston*, I wasn't sure I was hearing anything. The four hours seemed to pass at a different speed from ordinary time — slower, and also somehow more spacious. I came out of the concert hall uncertain whether I had experienced something or simply endured it.

It took several more hearings to understand that this uncertainty was the point.

## What slow music asks

Music that unfolds slowly asks something specific of the listener: not attention exactly, because attention implies effort, a kind of gripping. What it asks is closer to availability — a willingness to let time pass differently, without pushing against it.

This is harder than it sounds. We are trained by most of our listening experiences to expect development, arrival, resolution. Music that declines to provide these things at the expected rate can feel, on first encounter, like a test of patience. It is only later that you realise patience is the wrong frame entirely.

## What you find on the other side

I have come to think that slow music — Feldman, Scelsi, Pärt, the late Schubert — creates a particular kind of interior space. The silence between sounds begins to feel inhabited. The repetition, instead of dulling, sharpens. Small differences that you would miss at ordinary listening speed become significant.

The experience is a little like adjusting to darkness. At first there seems to be nothing there. Then, gradually, the room fills.
